content: Do you think she meant it?"
Falk pondered; after a while he resumed the conversation.
I should take jolly good care not to."
"At present only mrs Homan, the controller's wife, and Lady Rehnhjelm."
I think I've heard it before.
I have bills on all with the exception of one."
"Hold your tongue and do as I tell you!
Of course you weren't, for there was no party.
No! Hold on! We'll postpone that!
I guarantee such and such a sum; I must pay it-that's clear!"
It was impossible; he dressed and went to Falander's house.
Rehnhjelm overwhelmed him with questions, but Falander refused to reply before ten o'clock.
"A spider on the morrow: grief and sorrow."
"I remember the book; I liked it very much.
"Here," he said, handing it to Agnes; "go home and rid the world of a monster."
"What's that?" laughed Agnes, opening the box and taking out a six barrelled revolver.
"Lock it up," she said, "this is no toy, my friends."
"I see, I have to speak more plainly.
You had supper with the manager!"
"Hadn't we better stop talking nonsense now and be off?
Put on your hats and come."
"Come along, let's be off," he said, spitting on the hearthstone.
"Poor devil!" muttered Sellen, covering him up with his rug.
"I want it, and a pair of fire tongs."
"But you can't do that sort of thing at Stockholm!"
Levin had watched the scene, quiet, neutral, and polite.
"That's Falk," said Sellen, opening the door.
Falk entered, looking a little hectic.
"Do you want money?" said Borg to the newcomer, laying his hand on his breast pocket.
"What a question to ask," said Falk, looking at him doubtfully.
I can let you have it."
"Good Lord, how modest you are," remarked Borg, and turned to Levin.
But how is it to be paid back?"
"Twelve crowns every sixth month, twenty four crowns per annum, in two instalments," said Levin promptly and firmly.
"That's no good; you are registered as assessor, and as such you still figure in the directory."
"One should be correct in matters of form," said Borg gravely.
"Don't do it, Falk," said Olle; "it'll end badly, there'll be trouble."
"I'll give you an hour," said Borg.
"If you are not back with the money by then, I'll set the police on your track."
"One takes up a fresh loan at the Tailors' Bank, for instance," replied Borg.
